summary: I was born in Aleppo, Syria in 1996, and it holds a special place in my heart as my birthplace and the home of my family. My father left Aleppo in 1967 to seek opportunities in the United States, and years later, in 2000, my mother and I left Aleppo to join him. Even though I moved at a young age, I have vivid memories of Aleppo and cherish the times I spent there during summers, which helped me understand the struggles my loved ones face. Aleppo is more than just a city to me; it represents my roots, my culture, and the home I long to return to one day.

Syria is my homeland, where I was born and where my family’s history is deeply rooted. I left Syria in 2000 with my mother to join my father in the United States, but I have always felt connected to Syria through my relatives, the culture, and the memories of home. The ongoing conflict there has been heartbreaking to witness from afar, especially knowing that my family and friends are enduring hardships and are often misunderstood or criticized globally. Despite the challenges, Syria will always be home to me, and I hold onto the hope of returning one day.

The United States has been my home since 2000, when my mother and I joined my father who had moved here in 1967. For my father, America represented the land of opportunity and the chance to achieve the 'American Dream' through hard work and stability. Growing up as a Syrian American has given me a unique perspective, allowing me to appreciate the freedoms here while understanding the struggles faced by Syrians back home. America provided me with opportunities and a safe environment, and I am grateful for the life it has allowed me to build.
